title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read an article from which six sentences have been removed. Choose from the sentences A–G the one which fits each gap (37–42). There is one extra sentence which you do not need to use.

content.title: Taking the Waters: Hot Springs and the Culture of Bathing
content.text: In a valley in the Japanese Alps, long-tailed snow monkeys descend from the forest every winter to soak in a natural hot spring. They have been doing this for generations, and researchers believe they do it partly to relieve stress. Humans, it turns out, have been pursuing the same idea for thousands of years. (37) [37] The therapeutic use of naturally heated water has been documented in cultures from ancient Rome to Japan to Iceland. [/37] || Japan's onsen culture is perhaps the world's most elaborate. The country has tens of thousands of registered hot springs, and visiting them is a national pastime rather than a luxury. (38) [38] Each onsen is considered unique, its water possessing a distinct mineral composition thought to benefit different aspects of health. [/38] The ritual of undressing, washing thoroughly before entering, and then soaking in silence is observed with genuine reverence. || The Romans exported their bathing culture across their empire. The great bath complexes — thermae — were not merely places to wash, but social and civic institutions where business was conducted, philosophers debated and athletes trained. (39) [39] At their peak, Rome's thermae could accommodate thousands of bathers simultaneously. [/39] The ruins of these structures, from Bath in England to Caracalla in Rome, still attract millions of visitors annually. || Modern spa culture draws on all these traditions while adding a commercial dimension. Luxury wellness retreats in Iceland, geothermal pools in New Zealand and hammams in Morocco all market themselves as offering not just physical relaxation, but mental restoration. (40) [40] Research in physiotherapy and sports medicine increasingly supports the benefits of hydrotherapy for muscle recovery and circulation. [/40] || Some communities have used hot spring tourism to revitalise struggling rural economies. Small towns in Japan, Iceland and the western United States have transformed themselves into thriving wellness destinations. (41) [41] The challenge is managing visitor numbers sustainably so that the natural springs themselves are not degraded. [/41] || Whether you are a snow monkey in the Japanese Alps or a stressed city worker on a long weekend, the appeal of sinking into warm, mineral-rich water is remarkably consistent. (42) [42] For a few hours at least, the world outside the water's edge seems very far away. [/42]

paragraphs.A: What was once dismissed as folk medicine now has a growing body of scientific evidence behind it.
paragraphs.B: These baths were a democratic space where senators and slaves might soak side by side.
paragraphs.C: Success has also brought its own set of problems, however.
paragraphs.D: The practice appears to be one of the rare habits shared by nearly every human civilisation.
paragraphs.E: Bathing in Japan is therefore approached not as a casual habit but as a mindful practice.
paragraphs.F: The natural mineral content varies from spring to spring, making the experience different in each location.
paragraphs.G: The ability of warm water to ease both physical tension and mental anxiety seems to be universal.

questions.explanation: D afirma que la práctica es compartida por casi todas las civilizaciones humanas, generalizando el argumento antes de hablar de culturas específicas.
F explica que el contenido mineral varía de manantial en manantial, apoyando la idea de que cada onsen es único en su composición.
B describe las termas como un espacio democrático donde senadores y esclavos se bañaban juntos, reforzando su carácter de institución cívica y social.
A señala que lo que antes se consideraba medicina popular ahora tiene respaldo científico, conectando con la investigación en fisioterapia mencionada.
C advierte que el éxito también trae sus propios problemas, introduciendo el reto de gestionar el número de visitantes de forma sostenible.
G afirma que la capacidad del agua caliente para aliviar la tensión física y mental parece ser universal, cerrando el artículo con la reflexión sobre su atractivo universal.
